Facts.text: Charles II and Louis the German sign the Oaths of Strasbourg, a pact uniting their armies in the fight against their older brother Lothar
Pope Benedict VIII crowns Henry II Holy Roman Emperor
Jewish Cardinal Pietro Pierleone elected as anti-pope Anacletus II
English Archbishop Thomas Cranmer declared a heretic
Dutch fleet of 69 ships reaches Pernambuco, Brazil
Holy Roman Emperor Leopold I chases Jews out of Vienna
Dutch governor Falck and King Keerthisiri Rajasinghe of Kandy sign Treaty of Batticaloa
"Stars & Stripes" arrives in a foreign port for the first time (France)
First US textile machinery patent is granted to James Davenport in Philadelphia
Apple parer patented by Moses Coats, Downington, Pennsylvania
Jean Victor Marie Moreau, French general who plotted to overthrow Napoleon in the Pichegru Conspiracy, arrested, and exiled to the United States
Karadjordje leads the First Serbian Uprising against the Ottoman Empire
Ras Marye of Yejju marches into Tigray and defeats and kills Dejazmach Sabagadis in the Battle of Debre Abbay
Texas is linked by telegraph to the rest of the United States with the completion of a connection between New Orleans and Marshall, Texas
Oregon admitted as 33rd state of the Union
Galena, 1st US iron-clad warship for service at sea, launched, Connecticut
Hartford Steam Boiler Inspection & Insurance Co issues 1st policy
Morehouse College organizes (Augusta Georgia)

Esther Morris appointed US' first female in Justice of the Peace in South Pass City, Wyoming, after previous justice, R.S. Barr, resigned to protest passage of Wyoming Territory's women's suffrage amendment in 1869
Seraph Young becomes the first woman to legally vote in the modern United States, two days after the Utah legislature passed a law allowing women the vote
1st US state bird refuge authorized (Lake Merritt, California)
Chilean troops occupy Antofagasta
1st state labor union legislation; New Jersey legalizes unions
Cubs sell Mike King Kelly to Boston for record $10,000
1st train load of fruit (oranges) leaves LA for east
Venus is both a morning star & evening star
Stanley Cup, Victoria Skating Rink, Montreal, Quebec: Winnipeg Victorias beat Montreal Victorias, 2-0
Theodor Hertzl's "Der Judenstaat" (The Jewish State) is published, proposing a Jewish homeland as a means of escaping anti-Semitism in Europe
US Congress begins using voting machines
Date of events in Australian movie "Picnic at Hanging Rock"
Russia responds to international pressure to free Finland by tightening imperial control over the country
1st US foxhound association forms in NYC
1st US submarines with diesel engines commissioned, Groton, Connecticut
Arizona was admitted to the Union as the 48th state
High Council of Labor forms in Hague Netherlands
USSR adopts New Style (Gregorian) calendar (originally Feb 1)
The Polish-Soviet War begins.
United Parcel Service forms
League of Women Voters forms in Chicago
Canadian 5 cent nickel coin is authorized
Fictional character Skeezix is discovered on the Wallet family's doorstep in Frank King's cartoon strip "Gasoline Alley"
Little Review faces obscenity charges for publishing "Ulysses," in New York
State of emergency crisis in Bavaria ends, NSDAP re-allowed
Per-Erik Hedlund leads a Swedish sweep of the medals in the 50k cross-country at the St. Moritz Winter Olympics; Gustaf Jonsson & Volgar Andersson take the minor medals
Spanish government of General Damasco Berenguer falls
NHL Ace Bailey Benefit Game: Toronto beats All-Stars 7-3 in Toronto
Karl Schäferof Austria wins his 2nd consecutive men’s figure skating Olympic gold medal at the Garmisch-Partenkirchen Winter Games in Germany
National Negro Congress organizes in Chicago
Norwegian speed skater Ivar Ballangrud sets Olympic record 17:24.3 to win the 10,000m at the Garmisch-Partenkirchen Winter Games; his 3rd gold medal from 4 events and 4th career Olympic gold
British merchant vessel fleet is armed
Carson McCuller's "Reflections in a Golden Eye" published
Cebrie Park in the Bronx renamed Halsey Street
German Afrika Korps lands in Tripoli, Libya
One millionth vehicle traverses the NY Midtown Tunnel
Japanese parachutists land near oil center Palembang, Sumatra
Rotterdam's Maas tunnel opens
The Polish resistance movement, the Home Army, is formed and will eventually become the largest resistance movement in occupied Europe
World War II: Battle of Pasir Panjang contributes to the fall of Singapore.
German offensive against US troops through de Faid-pass, Tunisia begins, starting the Battle of Sidi Bou Zid
The Soviet Union recaptures the city of Rostov-on-Don, liberating Russia from the German 17th Army during WWII
Anti-Japanese revolt on Java
Carl Wick publishes "Salmon Trolling for Commercial & Sport Fishing"
Peru, Paraguay, Chile & Ecuador joins UN
World War II: Mostar is liberated by Yugoslav partisans.
World War II: Prague is bombed probably due to a mistake in the orientation of the pilots bombing Dresden.
1st session of Knesset (Jerusalem Israel)
Dutch Drees government presents plan for the building of 30,000 houses
Moroney scores cricket twin centuries for Australia at Johannesburg
USSR & China sign peace treaty
Giant slalom event for women debuts at the Winter Olympics at Oslo Games; American skier Andrea Mead-Lawrence wins gold ahead of Dagmar Rom of Austria and German Annemarie Buchner
VI Winter Olympic Games open at Oslo, Norway
"Trionfo di Afrodite (Triumph of Aphrodite)", the final work of Carl Orff's Trionfi trilogy premieres at La Scala Teatro in Milan, Italy
WTOC TV channel 11 in Savannah, GA (CBS) begins broadcasting
WFLA (now WXFL) TV channel 8 in Tampa-St Petersburg, Florida (NBC) begins
20th Congress of CPSU opens in Moscow
Indonesia withdraws from Netherlands Indonesian Union
Verhoeven, Nauta and De King/Wijnhout win Dutch 11 city skate
Georgia Senate unanimously approves Senator Leon Butts' bill barring blacks from playing baseball with whites
Arab Federation of Iraq & Jordan forms
$3.6 million heroin seizure in NYC
2nd Daytona 500: Junior Johnson survives a 37 car crash on Turn 4 to win driving a John Masoni owned Chevrolet
Marshal Ayub Khan elected President of Pakistan

Element 103, Lawrencium, is first produced in Berkeley, California, by bombarding a three-milligram target of three isotopes of californium with boron-10 and boron-11 nuclei from the Heavy Ion Linear Accelerator
US launches communications satellite Syncom 1
7th Daytona 500: Fred Lorenzen wins driving for Holman-Moody; race ends on lap 133 due to persistent rain
Australian currency is decimalised, and decimal currency postage stamps introduced
Russian writers Andrei Sinyavsky & Joey Daniel found guilty under the offense of anti-Soviet agitation and propaganda for publishing their satirical writings abroad
Latin American nuclear free zone proposal drawn up
Norwegian 15k Olympic cross country champion Harald Grønningen wins his second gold medal of the Grenoble Winter Olympics in the 4 x 10k relay
Soviet Union goes 1-2 in pairs figure skating at the Grenoble Winter Olympics; husband & wife team Oleg Protopopov and Liudmila Belousova edge teammates Tatiana Zhuk and Alexander Gorelik
WHKY TV channel 14 in Hickory, NC (IND) begins broadcasting
Movie "Ben Hur" is first shown on television
Tehran agreement signed; oil companies accept 55 percent tax rate, immediate increase in posted prices, and further successive increases
CBS "Late Movie" premieres
Lord Widgery arrives in Coleraine, where the 'Bloody Sunday' (30 January 1972) Tribunal was to be based, and holds a preliminary hearing
Luna 20 (Russia) launched to orbit & soft landing on Moon
Right-wing radicals plant a bomb at Amsterdam's under construction Venserpolder metro station, hoping to turn public sentiment against those protesting the building of the subway system
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
First "micro on a chip" or microchip is patented by Texas Instruments
In Kabul, Muslims kidnap the American ambassador to Afghanistan, Adolph Dubs who is later killed during a gunfight between his kidnappers and police
US launches Solar Maximum Mission Observatory to study solar flares
Stardust Disaster: A fire in a Dublin nightclub kills 48 people
"Night of 100 Stars", an all-star variety special celebrating the centennial of the Actors' Fund of America, takes place at NYC's Radio City Music Hall
Canadian speed skater Gaétan Boucher takes out the 1,000m gold medal at the Sarajevo Winter Olympics; also wins 1,500m; narrowly beats Soviet skater Sergey Khlebnikov in both events
Hostage CNN reporter Jeremy Levin is released in Beirut
Detroit Pistons-Philadelphia 76ers game draws crowd of 53,745 at Pontiac Silverdome, Pontiac; third largest crowd in NBA history; Pistons win, 125-107

30th Daytona 500: Bobby Allison beats his son, Davey, to the finish line; remembered forRichard Petty's rollover crash in the tri-oval on lap 106, rolls over 8 times and hit by Brett Bodine; walks away unhurt
African National Congress (ANC) opens office in Amsterdam
The first of 24 satellites of the Global Positioning System is placed in orbit
Union Carbide agrees to pay $470 mill damages for Bhopol disaster
World's 1st satellite Skyphone opens
Perrier recalls 160 million bottles of sparkling water after traces of benzene, a carcinogen, are found in some
Space probe Voyager 1 takes a photograph of the entire solar system, the images transmitted in real-time with earth a 'pale blue dot'
NL Cy Young winner Doug Drabek wins record $3 mil salary arbitration
American sprinter Andre Cason runs world record 60m indoor (6.41s) in Madrid, Spain
Cease fire in Somalia begins
Kieren John Perkins swims world record 800m freestyle (7:46.60)
Merlene Ottey runs world record 60m indoor (6.96 sec)
Fire in Linxi Department Store in Tangshan, China, kills 79
Authorities in the United States announce that Eric Robert Rudolph is a suspect in an Alabama abortion clinic bombing
Spacecraft NEAR Shoemaker enters orbit around asteroid 433 Eros, the first spacecraft to orbit an asteroid
Fishing vessel Tullaghmurray Lass sinks off the coast of Kilkeel, County Down, Northern Ireland killing three members of the same family on board
Roof of the Transvaal water park in Moscow collapses, killing more than 25 people and injuring more than 100 others
Lebanon's former Prime Minister Rafik Hariri is assassinated, prompting the country to fall into chaos
Online video-sharing site YouTube is founded in San Mateo, California, by Steve Chen, Chad Hurley, and Jawed Karim

Seven people are killed and 151 wounded in a series of bombings by suspected Al-Qaeda-linked militants in the Philippines that hit the Makati financial district in Metro Manila, Davao City, and General Santos City
A gunman opens fire in a lecture hall at Northern Illinois University in DeKalb County, Illinois, killing six people, including himself, and injuring 18
52nd Daytona 500: Jamie McMurray, driving for Earnhardt Ganassi Racing wins; Mark Martin, oldest polesitter in Daytona 500 history at 51 years, 27 days
America's Cup: Team USA defeats Alinghi 2-0 to win in Spain
Bahraini uprising begins in Bahrain, calling for greater political freedom and equality for the Shia population and later an end to the monarchy
A suicide bombing kills 4 police officer and injures 5 in Dagestan
Japan remains in recession as the economy shrinks by a further 0.1%
Oscar Pistorius, a South African amputee sprinter, is charged with the murder of Reeva Steenkamp
The Large Hadron Collider (LHC), the world's largest and highest-energy particle accelerator, will be switched off for two years for upgrading
The recession in the Eurozone economy deepens with a fall of 0.6% in GDP in the fourth quarter of 2012
Britain's Lizzy Yarnold wins her first of 2 consecutive Olympic women's skeleton gold medals in Sochi; also wins event in Pyeongchang (2018)
Italian Prime Minister Enrico Letta resigns after less than a year in office
Swiss cross country skier Dario Cologna wins his 2nd gold medal of the Sochi Winter Olympics in the 15k classical; also wins 30k skiathlon
Dutch speed skater Jorien ter Mors wins her 3rd career Olympic gold medal in the 1,000m at Pyeongchang; 1,500m and team pursuit champion in Sochi (2014)
Ex-student Nikolas Cruz shoots and kills 17 people at Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School, Florida, before being captured
Amazon cancels plans to build a corporate campus in Long Island, Queens, New York after widespread opposition
Pope Gregory VII excommunicates Holy Roman EmperorHenry IVfor the first time
Treaty of Dordrecht establishes an anti-French alliance between Holy RomanEmperor Maximilian Iand KingHenry VIIof England
Holy Roman EmperorCharles Venters Ghent without resistance and executes the rebels
Akbar, aged 13, is crowned the 3rd Mughal Emperor, succeeding his late father Humayun at Kalanaur in the Punjab

Peruvian nobleman Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala writes to offer Spanish KingPhilip IIIhis newly completed Chronicle of Andean history and Spanish rule - now only indigenous Andean viewpoint to survive (Danish National Library)
English parliament placesMary Stuartand PrinceWilliam IIIon the throne
Henry Pelhambecomes Britain's First Lord of Treasury
AstronomerJames Bradleypresents his discovery of the wobbling motion of the Earth on its axis to the Royal Society, London
US Congress commissionsBenjamin Lincolna major general in the Continental Army
Chief JusticeJohn Marshalldeclares that any act of U.S. Congress that conflicts with the Constitution is void
John C. Frémontand his party become the first Europeans to discover Lake Tahoe during his second expedition through the American West
Henry John Heinzfounds F & J Heinz with his brother John Heinz and cousin Frederick Heinz
Oscar Wilde's play "Importance of Being Earnest" premieres at St James's Theatre in London
Austro-Hungarian ZionistTheodor Herzlpublishes "Der Judenstaat" (The Jewish State), encouraging Jews to purchase land in Palestine
George Lohmanntakes a hat-trick for England v South Africa, 8-7 for inning - their lowest ever
PresidentTheodore Rooseveltsigns bill into law establishing US Department of Commerce and Labor
Thomas J. WatsonSr. renames the Computing-Tabulating-Recording Company (CTR) as International Business Machines (IBM)
Clas Thunbergof Finland wins his 2nd gold medal of the Games and 5th career title when he takes out the 1,500m speed skating at the St. Moritz Winter Olympics
The original "Dracula" film starringBela Lugosias the titular vampire is released
The George Burns andGracie AllenShow debuts on CBS radio, airing there until 1937, moving to NBC until 1949, and returning to CBS in 1949, before moving to television
Victor FlemingreplacesGeorge Cukoras director of film "Gone With The Wind"
Sugar Ray Robinsondefeats Jake LaMotta & takes middleweight title
SenatorJohn F. Kennedyappears on "Meet the Press"
Southern Christian Leadership Conference organizes in New Orleans withMartin Luther King Jr.as its President
US first ladyJacqueline Kennedyconducts a White House tour on TV
"8½" film directed byFederico Fellini, starring Marcello Mastroianni and Claudia Cardinale, is released (Academy Awards Best Foreign Language Film 1964)
British pop music duo Peter & Gordon release single "A World Without Love" (written byPaul McCartney, credited to Lennon-McCartney)
Wilt Chamberlainbreaks NBA career scoring record at 20,884 points
Aretha Franklinrecords her single "Respect" at Atlantic Studio in NYC; her rendition ofOtis Redding's tune becomes Billboard Song of the Year (1967)
13th Daytona 500:Richard Pettywins his 3rd Great American Race; goes on to win the inaugural Winston Cup title
PresidentRichard Nixoninstalls a secret recording system in the White House
John LennonandYoko Onoco-host the syndicated TV show "TheMike DouglasShow" for an entire week, in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Muhammad Alibeats British heavyweight boxing champion Joe Bugner by unanimous points decision in 12 rounds at Las Vegas Convention Centre
Soviet cross-country skierNikolay Zimyatovwins first of 3 gold medals at the Lake Placid Winter Olympics when he takes out 30k event; also wins relay and 50k gold
24th Daytona 500:Bobby Allisonwins with margin of victory overCale Yarboroughan impressive 23 seconds, more than half a lap

Britain'sJayne TorvillandChristopher Deanfamously dominate ice dancing at the Sarajevo Winter Olympics, performing toMaurice Ravel's "Bolero" in their free dance routine and recording 9-of-9 perfect scores for artistic impression
"Whitney Houston" debut album by Whitney Houston is released (Grammy Award Best Pop Vocal Performance, Female 1986, Billboard Album of Year 1986)
Alfredo Stroessnerre-elected President of Paraguay
Supreme Leader of Iran,Ayatollah Khomeiniissues a fatwa calling for the death ofSalman Rushdieand his publishers due to his novel "Satanic Verses". A bounty is also placed on his head.
"The Silence of the Lambs" film based on the book by Thomas Harris, directed by Jonathan Demme and starringJodie FosterandAnthony Hopkins, is released (Best Picture 1992)
Comedy film "Wayne's World" starringMike Myersand Dana Carvey, based on their Saturday Night Live sketch premieres
35th Daytona 500:Dale Jarrett, driving forJoe GibbsRacing, wins fromDale Earnhardtand Geoff Bodine
2nd ESPY Awards:Barry Bonds, Julie Krone win
Portland Trail Blazers tradeClyde Drexlerto the Houston Rockets, who end up winning the 1995 NBA Championship

"Live by Request" debuts on the A&E Network, based on singerTony Bennett's idea, the set list was determined by viewer phone calls; Bennett starred in the first episode, wins an Emmy Award (Individual Performance in a Variety or Music Program)
41st Daytona 500:Jeff Gordonwins; first Daytona 500 pole sitter to win the race sinceBill Elliottin 1987
8th ESPY Awards:Tiger Woods, Mia Hamm win
Alexandre Bilodeauwins the gold medal in the freestyle skiing men's moguls event, becoming the first Canadian to win gold during an Olympic Games hosted in Canada
ActressEllen Pagecomes out as gay in a speech at the Human Rights Campaign's "Time to Thrive" conference
For the first time in men's figure skating, all 3 Olympic singles medalists are of Asian descent;Yuzuru Hanyu(Japan) gold, Patrick Chan (Canada) and Denis Ten (Kazakhstan)
Laureus World Sports Awards, Monte-Carlo Sporting, Monaco: Sportsman:Usain Bolt; Sportswoman:Simone Biles; Team: Chicago Cubs
American snowboarderShaun Whitewins an unprecedented 3rd Olympic halfpipe gold medal (previous 2006, 10) at the Pyeongchang Winter Games
South African PresidentJacob Zumaresigns from office

Black Death Massacre: 900 Jews are burned alive in Strasbourg and a similar number are banned from the city after being blamed for the spread of the Black Death, part of a wave of pogroms across Western Europe

Battle of Cape St. Vincent: British fleet under Admiral Sir John Jervis defeats larger Spanish fleet under Admiral Don José de Córdoba y Ramos near Cape St. Vincent, Portugal. CaptainHoratio Nelsondistinguishes himself.
In New York City,James Knox Polkbecomes first serving US President to have his photograph taken (by Matthew Brady).
Alexander Graham Belland Elisha Gray apply separately for telephone patents, the Supreme Court eventually rules Bell the rightful inventor
Saint Valentine's Day Massacre in Chicago, seven gangsters are killed, allegedly onAl Capone's orders
Second day of the bombing of Dresden by Allied air forces

Leon Battista Alberti, Italian Renaissance humanist author (Dinner Pieces), architect (Tempio Malatestiano; Palazzo Rucellai; Santa Maria Novella), sculptor and cryptographer considered a founder of Western cryptography, born in Genoa, Republic of Genoa (d. 1472)
Pandolfo Petrucci, Italian ruler of the Republic of Siena (1500-12) during the Renaissance, born in Siena, Italy (d. 1512)
Johann Werner, German mathematician, born in Nuremberg, Germany (d. 1522)
Henry II of Bavaria, Bishop of Utrecht (1524-29), Worms (1523-52) and Freising (1541-52), born in Heidelberg, Germany (d. 1552)
Domenico Maria Ferrabosco, Italian Renaissance composer and singer, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1574)
Frederick III, the Pious, Elector Palatine of the Rhine, born in Simmern, Germany (d. 1576)
Christianus Adrichomius, Dutch Catholic priest and writer (Vita Jesu Christi), born in Delft, Netherlands (d. 1585)
Johann Pistorius, German theologist and historian, born in Nidda, Germany (d. 1608)
Hans Christoph Heyden, German composer, organist and poet, born in the Free Imperial City of Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1617)
Pier Francesco Cavalli, Italian opera composer, born in Crema, Lombardy, Republic of Venice (d. 1676)
John Wilkins, English Anglican clergyman and Bishop of Chester (1668-72), born in Fawsley, England(d. 1672)
Sybilla Schwarz, German baroque poet, born in Greifswald, Germany (d. 1638)
Georg Gabriel Schutz, German composer, born in the Free Imperial City of Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1716)
Georg Friedrich Kauffmann, German composer, born in Ostramondra, Thuringia, Electorate of Saxony (d. 1735)
John Sidney, 6th Earl of Leicester, English privy councillor, born in Penshurst Place, Kent (d. 1737)
Pierre-Claude Nivelle de La Chaussée, French writer (L’École des Mères), born in Paris (d. 1754)
Enrique Florez, Spanish historian, born in Valladolid, Spain (d. 1773)
Richard Allen, 1st African American ordained by Methodist-Episcopal church, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1831)
Jean Victor Marie Moreau, French general, born in Morlaix, Brittany, France (d. 1813)
Fernando Sor, Spanish classical guitarist, and composer (Twelve Studies), baptized in Barcelona, Spain (d. 1839)
Heinrich Baermann, German clarinet virtuoso and composer, born in Potsdam, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1847)
Emory Washburn, 22nd Governor of Massachusetts (1854-55), born in Leicester, Massachusetts (d. 1877)
Otis Tufts, American machinist and inventor who built printing machines, steam engines, firefighting equipment and invented the steam pile driver, born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 1869)
Lajos, Count Batthyany, 1st Prime Minister of Hungary, born in Pozsony, Kingdom of Hungary, Austrian Empire (d. 1849)
Alfred Thomas Agate, American artist, born in Sparta, New York (d. 1846)
Alexander Sergeyevich Dargomyzhsky, Russian composer, born in Troitsko village, Tula, Russia (d. 1869)
John McNeil, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada (d. 1891)
James Green Martin, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Elizabeth City, North Carolina (d. 1878)
Winfield Scott Hancock, American politician and Major General (Union Army), born in Montgomeryville, Pennsylvania (d. 1886)
Edmond François Valentin About, French writer (Le Roi des montagnes), born in Dieuze, Lorraine (d. 1885)
Alfred Iverson Jr., Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Clinton, Georgia (d. 1911)
Francois Haverschmidt, Dutch writer (Snikken en grimlachjes), born in Leeuwarden, Netherlands (d. 1894)
Margaret E. Knight, American inventor (flat-bottomed paper bag), described as a "female Thomas Edison", born in York, Maine (d. 1914)
Cecil De Vere, English chess player (1st official British chess champion (1866), born in London, England (d. 1875)
Julian Scott, American artist and Civil War Medal of Honor recipient, born in Johnson, Vermont (d. 1901)
Benjamin Baillaud, French astronomer, born in Chalon-sur-Saône, France (d. 1934)
Jan van Rijswijck, Flemish journalist and Mayor of Antwerp, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1906)
Frank Harris, Irish-American journalist and writer (My Life & Loves), born in Galway, Ireland (d. 1931)
C. B. Hawley, American organist and composer, born in Brookfield, Connecticut (d. 1915)
Joseph Thomson, Scottish geologist and early African explorer (Thomson's Gazelle), born in Penpont, Dumfries, Scotland (d. 1895)
George Washington Gale Ferris Jr., American engineer and inventor (Ferris Wheel), born in Galesburg, Illinois (d. 1896)
Eugen Schiffer, German lawyer and liberal politician, born in Breslau, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1954)
Waldemar Lindgren, Swedish-American Geologist (economic geology, ore classification), born in Vassmolösa, Kalmar, Sweden (d. 1939)
Israel Zangwill, Jewish author (Children of The Ghetto) and Zionist leader, born in London (d. 1926)
Robert E. Park, American sociologist (human ecology, marginal man), born in Harveyville, Pennsylvania (d. 1944)
Henricus Andreas Poels, Dutch Roman Catholic theologist and social foreman, born in Venray, Netherlands (d. 1948)
Charles Wilson, Scottish physicist and meteorologist (Wilson cloud chamber-Nobel), born in Glencorse, Scotland (d. 1959)
Bob Quinn, American baseball executive (owner Boston Red Sox 1923–33; part owner Boston Braves 1936–45; President National Baseball Hall of Fame 1948–51), born in Columbus, Ohio (d. 1954)
Gerda Lundequist, Swedish actress (The Saga of Gösta Berling), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1959)
Julius Nieuwland, Belgian-American chemist (inventor of 1st synthetic rubber neoprene), born in Hansbeke, Belgium (d. 1936)
Aida Overton Walker, African-American dancer and singer (In Dahomey; Salome), born in Richmond, Virginia (d. 1914)
Edgar Bainton, British-Australian composer (And I Saw a New Heaven; The Vindictive Staircase), and educator, born in Hackney, London, England (d. 1956)
Otto Selz, German psychologist, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1943)
William John Gruffydd, Welsh poet and scholar, born in Gorffwysfa, Bethel, Wales (d. 1954)
George Jean Nathan, American editor, author and critic (American Mercury), born in Fort Wayne, Indiana (d. 1958)
Ignaz Friedman, Polish pianist and composer, born in Podgórze, Kraków, Austrian Poland (d. 1948)
Hezekiah M. Washburn, American Presbyterian Congo missionary, born in Brooksville, Bracken, Kentucky (d. 1972)
Kostas Varnalis, Greek poet, born in Burgas, Bulgaria (d. 1974)
Syed Zafarul Hasan, Pakistani prominent Muslim philosopher, born in Pakistan (d. 1949)
Theodor Werner, German painter, born in Jettenburg, Germany (d. 1969)
Nina Hamnett, Welsh artist and writer, born in Tenby, Pembrokeshire, Wales (d. 1956)
Nikolaj A. Orloff, Russian-British pianist (Chopin), born in Yelets, Oryol Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1964)
Radola Gajda, Czech military commander and politician, born in Kotor, Austria-Hungary (d. 1948)
Kay Fisker, Danish architect (Hornbeck House, Copenhagen), born in Frederiksberg, Denmark (d. 1965)
Edward Arthur Milne, English astrophysicist (kinematic relativity), born in Yorkshire, England (d. 1950)
Jørgen Bentzon, Danish composer (Racconto; Saturnalia), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1951)
Fritz Zwicky, Swiss astronomer (supernova, dark matter), born in Varna, Bulgaria (d. 1974)
John Herman Randall Jr., American philosopher (Western Man), born in Grand Rapids, Michigan (d. 1980)
Lovro von Matačić, Croatian conductor and composer (Bruckner Medal), born in Sušak, Croatia (d. 1985)
Jessica Dragonette, Italian-American radio singer, and honorary US Army colonel, born in Calcutta, British India (d. 1980)
Fred Scott, American actor and singing cowboy, known as "The Silvery-Voiced Buckaroo"  (Melody of the Plains), born in Fresno, California (d. 1991)
Ray "Crash" Corrigan, American actor (Crash Corrigan's Ranch), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 1976)
Thelma Ritter, American character actress (All About Eve; Rear Window; Pillow Talk), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 1969)
Fritz Büchtger, German composer, educator, and modern music proponent (Association for Contemporary Music; Studio for New Music), born in Munich, Germany (d. 1978)
Stuart "Stu" Erwin, American actor (The Stu Erwin Show), born in Squaw Valley, California (d. 1967)
Charles Oatley, English electrical engineer (developed scanning electron microscope), born in From, England (d. 1996)
Hertha Kuusinen, Finnish communist politician (daughter of Otto Ville Kuusinen), born in Luhanka, Finland (d. 1974)
Florence Rice, American actress (Fast Company, Double Wedding, Riding on Air), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1974)
A. M. Klein, Canadian modernist Jewish poet (Hath Not a Jew...), born in Ratno, Ukraine (d. 1972)
Willem J. Kolff, Dutch-American Physician and Biomedical Engineer (artificial kidney, hemodialysis), born in Leiden, Netherlands (d. 2009)
Edmund G. Love, American historian and author (A Small Bequest, Subways Are for Sleeping), born in Flushing, Michigan (d. 1990)
Juan Pujol Garcia, Spanish double agent (spied for Britain, codename Garbo, pretended to spy for Nazi Germany codename Arabel), born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 1988)
Ollie Harrington, American cartoonist (Bootsie), born in Valhalla, New York (d. 1995)
Tibor Sekelj, Croatian polyglot, explorer and writer ("Kumewawa, the son of the jungle") , born in Spišská Sobota, Austria-Hungary (d. 1988)
Ab Visser, Dutch poet and writer (Man Without a Head), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1982)
James Pike, American Episcopal controversial bishop (Beyond Anxiety), born in Oklahoma City (d. 1969)
Jimmy Hoffa, American Union leader (Leader of Teamsters Union with ties to organized crime, disappeared mysteriously in 1975), born in Brazil, Indiana (d. 1975)[1]
Mel Allen, American sportscaster (voice of NY Yankees; 22 World Series; 24 MLB All-Star games), born in Birmingham, Alabama (d. 1996)
Woody Hayes, American College Football Hall of Fame coach (National C'ship 1954, 57, 61, 68, 70; Ohio State), born in Clifton, Ohio (d. 1987)
Norman Von Nida, Australian golfer, coach, broadcaster (British Open 1948 3rd), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 2007)
Irving Gordon, American songwriter ("Unforgettable"; "Me, Myself, And I"), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 1996)
Edward Platt, American actor (Get Smart - "The Chief"; North By Northwest), born in Staten Island, New York (d. 1974)
Marcel Bigeard, French general (WWII, First Indochina War, Algerian War), politician, and writer, born in Toul, France (d. 2010)
Masaki Kobayashi, Japanese film director and screenwriter (Joi-uchi), born in Otaru, Japan (d. 1996)
Wawrzyniec Żuławski, Polish composer, born in Zakopane, Poland, Russian Empire (d. 1957)
Herbert A. Hauptman, American mathematician whose models for determining the chemical crystal structures vital for life revolutionized chemistry (Nobel 1985), born in New York City (d. 2011)
Red Hickey, American football coach (HC SF 49ers 1959-63; devised shotgun formation), born in Clarksville, Arkansas (d. 2006)
Hugh Downs, American TV journalist (20/20, Concentration), born in Akron, Ohio (d. 2020)
Jeanne Demessieux, French organist and composer (Te Deum), born in Montpellier, France (d. 1968)
